text: Hey everyone, I'm Shaw. In this video, I'm going to discuss how to create a custom MCP server and connect it to ChatGpt. I'll start by reviewing key MCP concepts and then go through a concrete example with code. So, at this point, you're probably familiar with the model context protocol or MCP for short, but just to recap, MCP is a standard way to connect tools to AI applications. You may have already seen MCP in action when using tools like Chatubt or Claude where you can integrate it with apps like Notion, Gmail, Google Calendar, Google Drive and so on and so forth. Ultimately, what this enables is for you to connect your favorite tools like notion and the like to your favorite AI applications. However, a more recent feature of apps like ChatBT and Claude is that you can now integrate them with custom tools. For example, you could create an MCP server that allows ChatBT to access YouTube transcripts given a video's URL. And since MCP is an open standard, if you build a MCP server that works with Chat GBT, it will instantly work with other apps like Claude. Building this MCP server and connecting it to ChatBT is exactly what we're going to do in this video. But first, to give a bit more background on how MCP works, it uses a so-called client server architecture, which is pretty standard in the web development world. The way this works is you'll have a so-called MCP client which will live inside an AI app like chat GPT. The client will send requests to so-called MCP servers. For example, it might ask a server, what tools do you have available? And then the server will send back a response listing all of its available tools. This is the basic communication protocol that allows chatpt to access say a custom MCPE server that gives it access to a set of tools and then if chatt wants to use one of these tools it can send a request in a particular format and then the MCP server will execute that tool call and send back the output. In previous videos, I've shown how to create custom MCP servers and to connect them to things like Claw Desktop or to build custom AI agents with them. However, one thing I have not covered in previous videos is how to set up authorization of an MCP server. In other words, restricting which applications and users can access your server. This is important when creating remote MCP servers. So in other words, servers that are running in the cloud because these are publicly available on the internet and potentially any application will be able to access your server and maybe that's not something you want to happen. So to restrict access to your MCP server running on the internet, there are three key concepts we need to know. The first is the MCP server, which is what we've already talked about. So this is the thing being accessed. This could be something like a YouTube MCP server. The next concept is the MCP client which we've talked about as well. This is the thing requesting access to the MCP server. So this could be something like CHBT. But what we haven't talked about is this third concept of an authentication server. This is a third component which is responsible for issuing access tokens after authenticating the client. Just to give you an idea of what this authorization flow can look like here, I'm going to walk through a specific example. Just a warning, this is going to get a little complicated. So if you're not really interested in how this O flow works, feel free to skip ahead to the example code. So the way this will work is that the MCP client will request access to this protected resource metadata. Basically, the client is asking the server for key information it needs to authenticate itself. So it'll send this request and the MCP server will send some metadata which will include a link to the authentication server. From there the client can then request the metadata from the authentication server. Then the server will send back the metadata. We can optionally have this dynamic client registration. Basically, instead of having every single app be preconfigured by the O server, we can just have this dynamic client registration which allows any client to register itself with the authentication server using this post request. Then the O server will return the client credentials. From here, the client will generate a few things to enable this OOTH flow and this will open up a little page in the browser so the user can sign into the O server. Once the user types in their username and password, it'll send this O request. The server will authorize the user. It'll redirect the user to the call back with the O code. Then the authorization code will get passed to the client which can now authorize itself by talking to the O server. The O server will give the client an access token which now enables the MCP client to send a request to the MCP server. The server will respond. So this is essentially like an initial handshake to kick off communication and then from here you can have the typical MCP communication. So the client will ask it to list its tools or maybe it will try to execute tool calls and then the server will respond accordingly.
